#ifdef ATT_OS_WINDOWS this macro in cpp chekc if it's defined or if it does has value of true?

The `#ifdef` directive in C++ checks whether a macro is defined, regardless of its value. If a macro is defined, `#ifdef` evaluates to true; otherwise, it evaluates to false.

If you also want to check the value of the macro, you can use a combination of `#ifdef` and conditional compilation using `#if` and `#endif` directives. Here's an example:

```cpp
#ifdef ATT_OS_WINDOWS
    #if ATT_OS_WINDOWS == 1
        // ATT_OS_WINDOWS macro is defined and has a value of 1
        // Your code here
    #else
        // ATT_OS_WINDOWS macro is defined but its value is not 1
        // Your code here
    #endif
#else
    // ATT_OS_WINDOWS macro is not defined
    // Your code here
#endif
```

In this example:
- If `ATT_OS_WINDOWS` macro is defined and has a value of 1, the code inside the `#if ATT_OS_WINDOWS == 1` block will be executed.
- If `ATT_OS_WINDOWS` macro is defined but its value is not 1, the code inside the `#else` block will be executed.
- If `ATT_OS_WINDOWS` macro is not defined, the code inside the `#else` block will be executed.

Remember to replace `// Your code here` with your actual code or logic based on the macro definition and value.
#if defined(__linux) || defined(__linux__) || defined(linux) this is deferent than #ifdef ?

The `#ifdef` directive in C++ checks if a macro is defined, regardless of its value. On the other hand, the `#if defined(...)` directive checks if a macro is defined and, additionally, allows you to check its value.

In your case, the macros you mentioned (`__linux`, `__linux__`, `linux`) are commonly used to identify Linux platform-specific code. Let's break down the differences between `#ifdef` and `#if defined(...)` for your specific case:

1. Using `#ifdef`:
```cpp
#ifdef __linux
    // Code here
#endif
```
This code checks if the macro `__linux` is defined, and if it is defined (regardless of its value), the code block will be compiled.

2. Using `#if defined(...)`:
```cpp
#if defined(__linux) || defined(__linux__) || defined(linux)
    // Code here
#endif
```
This code uses `#if defined(...)` to check if any of the specified macros are defined. If any of the macros `__linux`, `__linux__`, or `linux` are defined (again, regardless of their values), the code block will be compiled.

So, in summary:
- `#ifdef` checks if a macro is defined.
- `#if defined(...)` is used to check if a macro is defined and allows you to check multiple macro definitions in one condition.

In your case, both `#ifdef` and `#if defined(...)` will accomplish the same result since they are checking if certain Linux-related macros have been defined.
#if defined(ATT_OS_WINDOWS) && !defined(FORCED_OSC) cna i use not '!' in macros?

Yes, you can use the `!` (logical NOT) operator in macros within conditional directives in C++. In your case, combining the `!` operator with `defined()` is a valid way to check if a macro is not defined.

Here is an example of using `!` in combination with `defined()` in a conditional directive:

```cpp
#if defined(ATT_OS_WINDOWS) && !defined(FORCED_OSC)
    // Code here
#endif
```

In this example:
- `#if defined(ATT_OS_WINDOWS)` checks if `ATT_OS_WINDOWS` is defined.
- `!defined(FORCED_OSC)` checks if `FORCED_OSC` is not defined.
- If both of these conditions are true, the code within the `#if` directive will be included during compilation.

So, using `!` in macros within conditional directives is a valid way to specify a logical NOT condition for checking if a macro is not defined.
cna i put things before #if macor ? like include files before it in same file?

No, you cannot put non-preprocessor directives, such as includes or regular C++ code, before a preprocessor directive like `#if`. The preprocessor directives in C++ must appear at the beginning of a line and cannot be preceded by any other code or comments in the same line.

For example, the following code will produce a compilation error:

```cpp
#include <iostream>  // This line contains code
#if defined(ATT_OS_WINDOWS) && !defined(FORCED_OSC)
    // Code here
#endif
```

However, you can place includes or other statements before the `#if` directive if they are on separate lines:

```cpp
#include <iostream>  // This line is fine
#include <string>    // Another include

#if defined(ATT_OS_WINDOWS) && !defined(FORCED_OSC)
    // Code here
#endif
```

So, in summary, you can have includes or other statements before a preprocessor directive as long as they are on separate lines and not part of the same line as the preprocessor directive.

Yes, the code snippet you provided is correct and common in CMake files. It is using the `target_compile_definitions` command to define compile definitions for a specific target in CMake.

```cmake
target_compile_definitions(${target} PRIVATE
    ATT_OS_${os_name}
    ATT_COMP_${comp_name}
    FORCE_OSC
)
```

In this code snippet:
- `${target}` is the name of the target for which the compile definitions are being set.
- `ATT_OS_${os_name}`, `ATT_COMP_${comp_name}`, and `FORCE_OSC` are the compile definitions that are being added for the target.
- The `PRIVATE` keyword specifies that these compile definitions are applicable only to the target `${target}` and not to other targets that may depend on it.

This CMake code is commonly used to customize compilation based on various conditions or flags like operating system, compiler, or specific definitions.
explain this git command git restore --staged <file>

The `git restore --staged <file>` command is used in Git to unstage changes that have been added to the staging area (index) but have not been committed yet. This command is often used when you want to remove changes from the staging area without discarding the changes in your working directory.

Here is a breakdown of how the `git restore --staged <file>` command works:

- `git restore`: This is the Git command used to restore files or changes.
- `--staged`: This option specifies that the restore operation should target the staging area (index) where changes are added before committing.
- `<file>`: This parameter specifies the file or files for which you want to unstage changes from the staging area.

When you run `git restore --staged <file>`, Git will unstage the changes made to the specified `<file>`, moving them back to the working directory. The changes will no longer be part of the upcoming commit.

It's important to note that `git restore --staged <file>` does not discard the changes in the file; it simply moves them back to the working directory while keeping the modifications intact. If you want to discard the changes entirely (both from the staging area and the working directory), you can use `git checkout -- <file>`.

If you want to unstage all changes in the staging area, you can use `git reset HEAD` without specifying a specific file.
